proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored official descriptions are specific and consistent: the game explicitly calls itself a 'First-Person-Puzzle-Metroidvania' with an interconnected world that opens up through newly found upgrades and abilities, plus heavy emphasis on exploration, secret areas, environmental puzzle solving, and progression-gated traversal. The text repeatedly frames combat as a relatively small, sometimes puzzle-heavy component, which supports metroidvania over an action-led archetype. Detective cases and mystery-solving are notable topical layers, but the core gameplay identity is still ability-gated exploration in a handcrafted interconnected world.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Near-identical gameplay identity: first-person puzzle-metroidvania, versatile ability unlocks, dense secrets, toy-world traversal, minimal hand-holding, and light combat.
Same series formula and almost the same audience expectation: gated first-person exploration, puzzle-heavy progression, constant optional secrets, and even less combat emphasis.
A strong first-person metroidvania benchmark with interconnected progression and ability-gated backtracking, though it is more combat-driven and less puzzle-centric than Supraworld.
Shares first-person exploration, traversal-tool unlocks, revisiting areas, and secret hunting, but its structure leans more action-adventure than puzzle-metroidvania and supports co-op.
Standalone Supraland follow-up with the same first-person puzzle-metroidvania structure, traversal abilities, secret density, and playful environmental problem-solving.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently and explicitly identify Marathon as a 'PvPvE survival extraction FPS' and a 'PvP extraction shooter.' The core loop is clearly stated: infiltrate/scavenge zones, fight rival players and hostile AI, loot valuables, exfil, then use stolen loot and season-long unlocks to improve future runs. Supporting details include solo or crew-based runs, in-progress lobby insertion, finite supplies, ranked play, moddable weapons, build crafting, vault space expansion, and persistent seasonal progression. This strongly matches extraction_shooter over generic FPS labels or arena/hero shooter near-misses.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: The clearest genre benchmark: first-person extraction raids, PvPvE threats, gear loss on death, prep/loadout strategy, and progression driven by successful exfils. More hardcore and sim-leaning than Marathon, but still an essential comp.

Very close on match structure and tension: squad-or-solo PvPvE runs, contested objectives, hostile AI, rival teams, and extraction under pressure. Slower weapons and horror-western flavor differ, but the core loop fits strongly.
Sci-fi PvPvE extraction shooter focused on scavenging, contracts, rival players, and extract-with-loot progression.
Persistent extraction FPS with tactical looting, hostile factions, squad play, and survival-oriented exfil runs.
Loot-and-extract structure with other-player threat, persistent stash progression, and high-stakes session-based runs.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently and concretely describe ARC Raiders as a third-person multiplayer extraction game: players enter distinct topside maps, scavenge loot, survive hostile machines and rival Raiders, then return to the underground hub/workshop to sell loot, craft, repair, upgrade stations, learn blueprints, level skills, and take trader quests. The text explicitly supports solo or up-to-3-player play, PvPvE pressure, repeated incursions with changing map conditions, and progression through gear, crafting, quests, and a branching skill tree. That combination strongly supports extraction_shooter as the core gameplay identity, with loot_shooter and tactical_shooter as secondary affinities rather than primary genre fits.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close on core loop: sci-fi PvPvE drops for loot, hostile creatures plus players, extraction, contracts, and between-run gear progression. First-person perspective keeps it just short of a same-tier match.

Strong third-person extraction fit with scavenging runs, bring-home loot, and shelter upgrading. Slightly looser because it is more PvP-centric and less defined by co-op PvPvE enemy ecology than ARC Raiders.

A foundational extraction-shooter comp with raids, stash, traders, quests, and high-stakes loot loss. It is a bit less aligned due to first-person hardcore mil-sim emphasis and a more punishing sim/RPG layer than ARC Raiders.

Clear PvPvE extraction structure with AI threats, stealth/noise management, and squad extraction tension. Less close because its boss-hunt objective structure, old-weapons gunplay, and harsher permadeath flavor differ meaningfully from ARC Raiders' broader loot-progression loop.
Third-person sci-fi extraction shooter with machine threats, scavenging runs, PvPvE pressure, and persistent gear/base progression between sorties.

Rejected due to taxonomy coverage mismatch rather than lack of gameplay evidence.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently define Minos as a maze-building roguelite centered on designing and redesigning labyrinth layouts, placing walls/gates/corridors, linking traps through pressure-plate chain reactions, and defending against invading adventurers across repeatable runs. The clearest gameplay identity is systemic construction and trap engineering rather than direct action combat, which aligns best with engineering_sandbox_sim. Roguelite run structure, procedural variation ('Every run brings new enemies, new traps'), and single-player premium replayability are all directly supported. Some presentation details like camera/perspective are not stated, so those fields are left blank.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Centered on building lethal maze-like outposts packed with traps and routing intruders through killboxes.
A roguelite twist on reverse dungeon defense where you prepare traps and monsters to stop invading heroes.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Store and aggregator copy consistently describe a third-person action-RPG structure: exploring the area around Zoz, helping townspeople through quests, real-time slash-and-dodge combat with a machete, companions with unique combat skills, cooking for buffs, bond-building with residents, and magical weapon/tool enhancement. The strongest fit is a fantasy action RPG with quest exploration and social/progression layers. 'Open world' is not stated outright, so confidence is moderate rather than maximal, but the overall gameplay identity aligns best with open_world_fantasy_action_rpg over alternatives like loot_action_rpg or monster_hunter_style_action_rpg, which lack strong loot-chase or boss-hunt evidence here.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Fishing remains the core loop and it shares a relaxing, narrative-driven structure with character interactions, though it is more explicitly RPG/progression-led than the anchor.

This supports a primary identity of traditional_fighter, with party_game as a secondary flavor due to chaotic multiplayer arenas, items, and friend-focused free-for-all play.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close on party-first PvP brawling: round-based chaos, items, interactive stages, local/online play, and casual-competitive free-for-all energy.
Excellent match for hazardous arena melee and knockout-driven party sessions, though its floppy physics focus is looser and less combo-structured than Prop Sumo.
Major benchmark for multiplayer arena fighting with items, stage hazards, free-for-alls, and custom rules; less close in perspective, movement model, and fighter precision.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored official descriptions consistently define The Occultist as a first-person narrative thriller with explicit survival-horror framing: 'Survival horror: The Occultist has all the distinctive elements of the genre,' combined with tense exploration, supernatural threats, ominous cult imagery, and a horror-focused abandoned-island setting. That strongly supports survival_horror as the primary identity, with psychological_horror and stealth_action_adventure as meaningful secondary overlaps.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Samson as a single-player open-world urban action game, not a pure racer. The core loop centers on moving through open world city districts, taking consequential jobs/missions under a debt-and-time-pressure structure, engaging in close-quarters brawler combat, and using cars as traversal/combat tools during chases and collisions. Repeated signals include 'Open World Districts,' 'Brawler Combat,' 'Cars as Weapons,' climbing through alleys and rooftops, a 25+ upgrade skill tree, law-response escalation, and story-driven progress tied to Samson's sister and criminal debt. Racing is present, but the evidence frames driving as one major verb inside a broader open-world action-adventure campaign rather than the sole governing loop.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Excellent fit: open-world city structure, story-driven crime missions, strong hand-to-hand combat emphasis, driving integrated into progression, and police heat all align closely with Samson’s stated loop.

That evidence supports a quest-driven open-world western narrative RPG more strongly than a pure shooter or action-adventure classification. proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Sigma Star Saga DX as a deliberate hybrid: top-down planetary RPG-style exploration combined with side-scrolling shoot-'em-up space battles, with branching story, multiple endings, boss battles, tool-based exploration, and weapon customization via Gun Data. proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently describes Scritchy Scratchy as a scratch-card incremental/idle-style progression game centered on buying scratch-offs, revealing symbols, unlocking auto-scratching, taking risks on higher-variance cards, and prestiging for permanent upgrades. That establishes a clear gameplay identity, but it does not map cleanly to any available Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype. It is not supported as pinball, racer, sports, card battler, management tycoon, or other listed archetypes, and forcing a fit would misclassify the title. Rejecting is appropriate because the game appears to sit outside the current archetype set rather than lacking evidence.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close on idle/incremental structure: manual actions become automation, numbers scale rapidly, and prestige/permanent upgrades drive long-term progression.
Strong fit through luck-driven payout loops, run-building synergies, and gambling-style variance, though it is more roguelite run-based than idle/prestige incremental.
Good neighboring comp for passive income, automation managers, offline growth, and reset-based progression, but its business-scaling loop is less tactile and less luck-variance driven.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently present DigDigDrill as a single-player underground mining and resource-collection game focused on digging for minerals and treasure, returning to the surface when inventory is full, and forging/customizing drills through a puzzle-like smithing/build-synergy system. The text explicitly says mining occurs 'without enemies or time limits,' which weakens any combat-driven archetype despite the 'hack-and-slash style equipment system' phrasing. The dominant loop appears to be relaxed resource gathering, build crafting, and progression through deeper floors, but the available Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype list does not contain a clear non-combat mining / digging / crafting progression archetype that this cleanly fits. Accepting would require forcing the game into a combat, survival, sandbox, or management identity not strongly supported by the evidence.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Best match for dig-deeper resource runs and upgrade-driven underground progression. It differs by being a combat-heavy platform metroidvania, but mining and equipment-led advancement still overlap strongly.
Shares underground mining, ore collection, crafting, and long-term power growth. Fit is loosened by its sandbox-survival/base-building focus, multiplayer profile, and much broader activity set.
2D descent-based mining loop with return-to-town upgrades and equipment-driven drilling progression.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Low-pressure dig-sell-upgrade structure focused on relaxing excavation and gear improvement.
Upgrade-driven drilling deeper for minerals with hauling and equipment progression at the core.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently identifies Defending Camelot as a tower defense game explicitly compared to Plants vs. Zombies, with 160 campaign levels, unit unlocking, coin collection, power-ups, and waves of monsters prevented from entering Camelot. That is strong evidence for tower defense gameplay, but tower defense is not represented by a suitable archetype in the provided Similar Games Taxonomy V2 list. Available archetypes like rts or side_scrolling_action_strategy would misclassify the core gameplay identity.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: The defining comp: lane-based tower defense, staged waves, varied defender roster, progression through many levels, and explicit audience overlap.
A very close tower defense match with campaign structure, unit/tower specialization, spell support, enemy-type counterplay, and long-form level progression.
Clearly in the same tower defense family, but its freer map interaction, top-down control, and co-op-focused presentation make it a somewhat looser fit than PvZ or Kingdom Rush.
Polished campaign tower defense with hero abilities, enemy counters, upgrades, and map-by-map progression.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently supports a non-combat, cozy single-player handcrafted puzzle game focused on coloring stained glass, rule-based logic puzzles, secret optional puzzles, and a level editor. However, the available Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetypes do not include a general logic/puzzle-game bucket. The system-generated near miss for hidden_object_puzzle is not well supported by the actual description, which emphasizes rule-based handcrafted puzzles rather than object-finding scenes. Because the strongest evidence indicates a puzzle identity that does not cleanly map to an allowed archetype, the title should be rejected rather than forced into an inaccurate classification.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Excellent fit on handcrafted solo puzzle progression, layered rule discovery, exploration, and optional secrets. Less cozy/artisanal and more open-world first-person, but the mastery curve is very aligned.
Strong match for relaxed, curated, non-combat puzzle play with elegant mechanics, discovery, and off-path optional content. Its push-block/island structure is different, but audience overlap is high.

Very good pure-logic comparison: hand-designed, rule-complete deduction with escalating challenge and no guessing. However, it lacks the anchor's cozy presentation, exploratory structure, and artistic restoration feel.
Stained-glass themed puzzle assembly directly matches the anchor’s art-restoration fantasy and relaxed solo play.
Cozy art-construction puzzler focused on filling patterns under simple constraints rather than reflex play or combat.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently frame the game as a single-player detective mystery built around examining documents, photographs, and artifacts, following leads, and making deductions with no hand-holding. Source labels include Point-and-Click, and the computed evidence strongly supports cursor-driven interaction, puzzle gating, environmental puzzle solving, authored linear structure, mystery-solving goals, and no combat. That aligns best with hidden_object_puzzle in this taxonomy, despite the game emphasizing deduction/investigation more than literal hidden-object scenes.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Store descriptions consistently present a business-management simulator loop: rent and sell movies, organize inventory, hire staff, manage returns and fees, decorate and expand the shop, and respond to seasonal customer demand. This strongly supports management_tycoon over city_builder/colony_sim because the focus is operating and optimizing a commercial venue rather than urban planning or autonomous colony survival. Evidence also clearly supports single-player, non-combat, sandbox-style progression with base expansion and sim-oriented challenges.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: First-person retail management with stocking shelves, cashier tasks, layout optimization, and gradual store expansion.
Task-driven first-person business sim where you directly serve customers, restock, hire workers, renovate, and expand services.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored store and aggregator copy consistently describes a train-driving simulation built around taking the controls of Thomas and other engines from an interactive first-person cab view. The game emphasizes carrying passengers, hauling freight, shunting, running timetable services, and a free-roam mode, with stories and exploration across Sodor. It is explicitly described as being powered by Train Sim World with realism/simulation technology but accessible controls, strongly supporting transport_sim over racing or adventure archetypes. Single-player only, non-combat, vehicle-control-centric, and simulation-driven structure are all directly supported by the provided text.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Same core first-person train-driving sim foundation, with scenario-based runs, timetable services, and accessible cab operation.
Another close match in Dovetail’s route-based train-sim format focused on driving services and curated scenarios from the cab.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored Steam and Metacritic text consistently and concretely describe a first-person survival horror campaign set in Sker Hotel where the player must stay hidden, manage breathing and noise, evade enemies that hunt by sound, solve puzzle mechanisms, explore for clues and notes, and use a limited defensive device only to stun enemies and escape. The copy repeatedly names the game 'survival horror' and emphasizes stealth, close-quarters tension, exploration, and multiple endings shaped by clues, which strongly supports survival_horror as the core gameplay identity. Stealth-action and psychological horror elements are present, but the dominant loop is survival-focused horror exploration and evasion rather than action combat.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Direct flat-screen counterpart with the same core loop: first-person stealth survival horror, sound-sensitive enemies, limited defensive device, clue-driven exploration, and multiple endings.
Story-driven VR horror with first-person exploration, vulnerability, and environmental interaction, but it is more psychological narrative horror than stealth-survival systems horror.

Close-quarters VR horror with investigation and object interaction fits the immersive vulnerability side, though its episodic exorcism/investigation structure is looser than Maid of Sker VR's stealth-evasion survival loop.
VR first-person survival horror in a claustrophobic hotel with puzzle exploration, scarce resources, and constant vulnerability.
VR stealth-horror focused on hiding, environmental interaction, puzzle solving, and surviving threats with little direct combat.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored Steam and corpus text consistently describe a top-down Survivor-style action roguelite built around 1-4 player co-op runs, fighting hordes of enemies, stacking upgrades each run, combining weapons and trinkets, and culminating in boss battles. The core loop is much closer to a co-op action roguelite than beat-em-up or RPG alternatives. Strong signals include explicit 'top-down roguelite action,' 'Survivor-style combat,' high replayability, solo and up-to-4-player co-op, unlockable upgrades that grow stronger every run, and diverse enemies plus epic boss battles. Fantasy kingdom/warrior framing supports high_fantasy and heroic tone.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Defining survivor-like loop of surviving swarms, stacking upgrades each run, and outlasting escalating enemy waves in compact arena maps.
Closer action-heavy survivor formula with active dodging, large enemy hordes, boss encounters, and deep build combinations across multiple characters.
Top-down co-op roguelike with hero-specific kits, melee/ranged combat, map objectives, and major boss showdowns anchoring each run.
Arena survival structure focused on hordes, loot/build growth, character differentiation, and periodic boss fights within replayable runs.
Fast top-down 1-4 player co-op roguelite emphasizing distinct heroes, real-time combat, synergistic upgrades, and boss-centric progression.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions are consistent and strong: this is a single-player survivors-like/top-down shoot-'em-up action roguelike built around repeated runs, endless enemy waves, bullet-hell dodging, procedurally generated weapons, stacking upgrades, and build-driven survival. However, the current Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype list does not contain a clean fit for a single-player survivors-like/top-down action roguelite shooter. Available shooter and roguelite archetypes are materially mismatched (for example, hero shooter, military FPS, tactical shooter, extraction shooter, loot shooter, or co-op action roguelite). Because the gameplay identity is clear but does not map reliably to an allowed archetype, this title should be rejected rather than force-fit.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Definitive wave-survival roguelite with escalating enemy swarms, rapid upgrade drafting, and run-based power snowballing.
Closest match on survivors-like structure, bullet-heavy arena chaos, build stacking, boss waves, and endless-style scaling.
Another compact arena survival roguelite centered on surviving waves through fast buildcraft, item synergies, and constant stat tuning.
Dark fantasy survivors-like focused on kiting huge hordes, ability synergies, and long-form progression within repeated survival runs.
More manual twin-stick shooting and dodging, but very similar in bullet-hell pressure, short runs, and weapon-centric upgrade builds.
